summary: High-risk regulatory changes impacting food seasoning/snack compliance: European Union introduces new microbiology limits for foodborne bacteria (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria) and special Salmonella guarantees for trade. GCC has new microbiological limits for foodstuffs and ingredients. Singapore has enhanced additive limits (requiring technological justification, no health risks, explicit compliance, and prohibition of unpermitted additives) and contaminant limits (explicit compliance, prohibition of specific veterinary drug residues). Singapore also mandates declaration of ingredients/additives for prepacked foods. Taiwan now requires mandatory allergen declaration for common allergens and sulphite (≥10 mg/kg). The United States added sesame as a major allergen (effective Jan 1, 2023), introduced new guidance/action levels for 3-MCPD (1 ppm) and Aflatoxins (20 ppb), and clarified mandatory labeling for Cochineal extract/carmine, FD&C Yellow No. 5, major food allergens (with specific declaration methods), 'gluten-free' claims, and sulfiting agent declaration (≥10 ppm). Thailand has new prohibitions on certain foods from production, import, or sale. Vietnam has new allergen declaration requirements. A high-severity monitoring alert for Japan indicates a source data extraction issue for labelling requirements, requiring manual review.

changes.parameter: Foodborne bacteria, toxins, and metabolites (e.g., Salmonella, Listeria monocytogenes, Enterobacter sakazakii, staphylococcal enterotoxins, histamine)
changes.notes: Food safety criteria for relevant foodborne bacteria, their toxins and metabolites must comply with Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.

For trade of certain live animals and products into Sweden, Finland, and Norway, special guarantees regarding Salmonella prevalence and control programs must be met, as laid down in specific Council Decisions and Regulations. Other EU countries can obtain similar guarantees if their control program is recognized as equivalent.
This technical regulation sets microbiological limits for foodstuffs and food ingredients. Specific limits must be verified by consulting the full standard.

changes.parameter: Crustacean shellfish, mango, peanuts, sesame seeds, milk and goat milk, eggs, tree nuts, gluten-containing cereals, soybeans, fish
changes.oldLimit: Mandatory declaration if present as ingredient or food additive
changes.newLimit: Mandatory declaration if present as ingredient or food additive
changes.notes: These are mandatory allergens requiring labeling.
Mandatory declaration if the concentration meets or exceeds the limit.

changes.newLimit: Prohibition from production, import, or sale
changes.notes: A list of foods prohibited from production, import, or sale must be verified.

The nine major food allergens recognized by U.S. law are milk, eggs, fish, Crustacean shellfish, tree nuts, peanuts, wheat, soybeans, and sesame. Sesame was added as the 9th major allergen effective January 1, 2023, by the FASTER Act.
Guidance Level for 3-MCPD in acid-hydrolyzed protein and Asian-style sauces.
Action Level for total aflatoxins in general foods.
Action Level for total aflatoxins in peanuts and peanut products.

changes.parameter: Chemical contaminants (tolerances, action levels, guidance levels)
changes.notes: Contaminant levels are published in various guidance documents and regulations for industry. Tolerances and action levels are defined in 21 CFR parts 109 and 509. The Chemical Contaminants Transparency Tool provides a consolidated list.

changes.parameter: Declaration of Cochineal extract and carmine
changes.notes: Color additives made from cochineal extract and carmine, identified as allergenic substances, must be declared on the label of all food and cosmetic products.
All products containing FD&C Yellow No. 5 must identify it on their labels due to potential hypersensitivity reactions in some individuals.

The food source of all major food allergens must be declared. This can be done in parentheses following the ingredient name (e.g., 'lecithin (soy)') or immediately after or next to the list of ingredients in a 'contains' statement (e.g., 'Contains wheat, milk, and soy.'). The type of tree nut, species of fish, and species of Crustacean shellfish must also be declared.
Various sulfiting agents must be declared on food labels when present in food and the concentration in the food is equal to or greater than 10 parts per million total sulfites.

The FDA issued a final rule defining 'gluten-free' for food labeling. On August 12, 2020, compliance requirements were established for fermented and hydrolyzed foods, or foods that contain fermented or hydrolyzed ingredients, bearing the 'gluten-free' claim.
Specific regulations for allergen declaration requirements in seasoning, snack food, and processed foods must be verified through official government sources.
This standard specifies the varieties, scope of use, and maximum dosages of food additives. Specific limits must be verified by consulting the full document.
The Codex General Standard for Food Additives (GSFA, Codex STAN 192-1995) sets forth conditions for permitted food additives in all foods. Provisions are available in a searchable online database.

For Ready-To-Eat (RTE) foods, shelf-life studies (durability studies and challenge tests) for Listeria monocytogenes must be conducted according to technical guidance, and sampling procedures for L. monocytogenes in processing areas and equipment must follow guidelines to comply with food safety criteria in Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.
Process hygiene criteria must be met to indicate the correct functioning of the production process, as laid down in Commission Regulation (EC) No 2073/2005.

Advisory statements (e.g., 'may contain [allergen]') are not required by law. They can be used to address unavoidable cross-contact only if manufacturers have incorporated good manufacturing practices and taken every precaution to avoid cross-contact. Such statements must be truthful and not misleading.
